food_subgroup: Crab-based noodle dish with chilli sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore Chilli Crab Vermicelli; closest parent 'crab dish' (FOODON_03311737) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chilli crab vermicelli; 'Crustaceans, cooked' (A0CJK) is the closest defensible parent category covering the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Singapore Chilli Crab Vermicelli or a sufficiently close parent dish concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Singapore Chilli Crab Vermicelli is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of crab pieces in chilli sauce served over a bed of rice vermicelli.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab with Bee Hoon (Rice Vermicelli) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Chilli Crab' and 'Bee Hoon (boiled)' as separate entries. Nutrient values for this composite dish are derived by combining HPB Foodo data for chilli crab (approximately 60% by weight) and boiled rice vermicelli (approximately 40% by weight), consistent with standard restaurant proportions. Energy and macros are weighted accordingly. This is the highest-priority approved source for Singaporean dishes.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chilli Crab Vermicelli as a composite dish. The GI is estimated at 53 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: rice vermicelli (bee hoon), which has a published GI of approximately 58 (University of Sydney GI Database). The chilli crab sauce and protein components lower the effective GI of the composite dish. A conservative estimate of 53 is applied, consistent with mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate meals. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (53 × 47.2 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 25.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chilli paste; a single serving may provide up to 73% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the rice vermicelli contributes a moderate glycemic load (~25 per serving); pairing with the protein and fat from crab and sauce moderates postprandial glucose response, but portion control remains essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from crab
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its high sodium content and moderate cholesterol (~68 mg per 100 g from crab), though the saturated fat content is relatively low; occasional consumption in small portions may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Chilli Crab Vermicelli is a Singaporean seafood noodle dish that combines the iconic flavours of Singapore's national dish — chilli crab — with rice vermicelli (bee hoon or mee hoon). The dish features mud crab cooked in a rich, semi-thick sauce made from blended chillies, tomato, garlic, ginger, shallots, egg, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, served over or alongside soft boiled rice vermicelli noodles. The sauce is simultaneously spicy, sweet, savoury, and tangy, with the egg stirred in to create silky ribbons throughout. The crab provides a sweet, firm, and slightly briny protein, while the vermicelli absorbs the sauce and provides a soft, slippery texture contrast. This dish is commonly found in Singaporean seafood restaurants and hawker-style establishments, and represents a practical adaptation of the classic chilli crab for diners who prefer a noodle accompaniment. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from crab (~8.2 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from vermicelli (~11.8 g per 100 g), and a relatively low saturated fat content (~0.9 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g), which makes it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category), making it moderately suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions. The dish contains crustacean, egg, soy, and gluten allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal dietary requirements without verified halal-certified preparation.

confidence_reason: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for Singapore Chilli Crab Vermicelli as a unified composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by combining HPB Foodo data for chilli crab and boiled rice vermicelli in estimated restaurant proportions (60:40 by weight). GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Thai', which is incorrect; this dish is Singaporean in origin and has been corrected to 'Singaporean'. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Singapore using a weighted combination of chilli crab and boiled bee hoon entries at approximately 60:40 weight ratio. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fresh seafood and vegetable-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source data. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 7. GI is estimated at 53 based on the rice vermicelli component (University of Sydney GI Database, GI ~58 for rice vermicelli); the composite dish GI is conservatively adjusted downward to 53 to account for protein and fat from crab and sauce. 8. Halal status is marked false as standard chilli crab preparations in Singapore may use non-halal ingredients; halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed. 9. Serving size of 400 g is based on typical restaurant portion for a shared crab dish with noodles. 10. Glycemic load per serving = (53 × 47.2) / 100 = 25.0, where 47.2 g is the available carbohydrate in a 400 g serving (11.8 g per 100 g × 4).

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Thai' (input error) to 'Singaporean' based on dish origin.
Added all five mandatory Singaporean local-language names: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Derived nutrient values from HPB Foodo using weighted combination of chilli crab and bee hoon entries.
Estimated GI at 53 from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli data with documented rationale.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-not-reported justification in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to permitted values: closest_parent and none.
